topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp parchment felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the official letter. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air that did little to settle the churning in my stomach. The words swam before my eyes, detailing the generous award, the full tuition coverage, the prestigious name attached to my name. Then, the fine print. A name listed beneath the scholarship recipient—a name that wasn't mine. A slow, hot pressure began to build behind my sternum, tightening with every line of text. I crumpled the paper in my fist, the sound a muffled rip in the quiet library. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the polished floor, and paced the narrow aisle, my jaw clenched so tightly my molars ached. Every glance at the empty desks around me felt like an accusation. Someone had played a cruel trick, and the sheer injustice of it made my hands tremble uncontrollably.

[story 2]

"This is a joke," I muttered, the words tasting metallic and sour on my tongue. I stared at the acceptance notification glowing on my screen, the digital affirmation of my future dissolving into ash. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk, the tension radiating up my forearms. I reread the section about eligibility, my eyes darting back and forth, searching for a misplaced comma, a typo that would somehow validate my own existence. When I found nothing, the frustration boiled over. I slammed my laptop shut with unnecessary force, the sound echoing sharply in the otherwise silent dorm room. I kicked at the rug, a sudden, jerky movement, my chest heaving as I tried to draw a deep, steadying breath, but the air felt too thin, too suffocating. The unfairness of it settled over me like a heavy, suffocating blanket.

[story 3]

I watched the name on the scholarship announcement, a strange, cold clarity washing over the initial shock. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a deep, corrosive sense of betrayal. My hands, resting on the cool surface of the wooden table, began to shake with a rapid, uncontrolled vibration. I pushed back from the desk, the movement stiff and jerky, like a poorly oiled machine. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself against the rising tide of internal turmoil. The world outside seemed too bright, too cheerful, a stark contrast to the bleak, hollow feeling blooming in my core. I turned back, my gaze sweeping over the room, landing on everything with a sharp, dissatisfied scrutiny, as if searching for the hidden mechanism of the deception.
